text: The surgeon performs a simple lysis of adhesions on an incarcerated ventral incisional hernia, with clear visualization of the tissue planes between the serosa of the bowel and the abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses a forced bipolar instrument in the left hand to gently grasp the bowel. The instrument is capable of a stronger grasp for handling mesh and suturing, and provides a safe energy source for operating on the bowel.

The surgical ports are positioned on the patient's left abdominal wall, lateral to the midclavicular line. After clearing adhesions from the abdominal wall, additional ports will be placed to facilitate the cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, separating substantial adhesions between the liver and abdominal wall, as well as the small bowel adherent to the liver edge. Using blunt movements and sharp dissection, the adhesions are carefully released without cautery near the bowel. The DaVinci Endoscope Plus provides a clear view of the dissection plane, facilitating safe and straightforward separation of tissues.

The surgeon begins to expose the medial part of the gallbladder wall during a robotic-assisted multiport cholecystectomy. The robotic arms are arranged in a preferred setup, visible in the surgical field.

The assistant uses a laparoscopic retractor to elevate the gallbladder over the liver, with the left segment of the liver mobilized from adhesions. Two additional ports are placed across the midline, including one through the hernia defect, facilitating the removal of the gallbladder and stones, and the introduction of mesh into the abdomen.

The surgeon dissects one-third of the way up the gallbladder fossa before applying clips. Three clips are placed on the cystic duct and three on the cystic artery, followed by transection of both structures using electrocautery.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the fossa and places it into an endocatch bag. The bag is then advanced through the port placed through the abdominal wall defect, avoiding the need for dilation of the port site. Subsequently, the mesh is advanced through the same defect into the abdomen.

The surgeon closes the ventral hernia defects using a force bipolar instrument for needle manipulation and suturing. An absorbable twelve-inch V-lock suture is employed to approximate the defects, which measure approximately fifteen centimeters in length. The pneumoperitoneum is gradually decreased to eight millimeters mercury to aid in suturing, allowing the abdominal wall to relax for better approximation.

The surgeon uses an 18 by 22 centimeter Ovitex biologic mesh with a permanent proline matrix, avoiding full synthetic mesh due to the clean contaminated nature of the case. Two separate zero-V-lock sutures are fixed at the far corners of the mesh. The mesh is initially tacked into place in the patient's right lower quadrant with a few suture throws, then repeated in the right upper quadrant to ensure proper centering.

The surgeon lifts the mesh to ensure it is centered and covers the defect appropriately. The mesh is then fixed to the patient's left upper quadrant using a new suture, with the top border kept taut. The suture line is continued down the left side of the abdomen, with the large piece of mesh visible in front of the camera.

The surgeon resumes fixing the lower border of the mesh in the patient's right lower quadrant, then moves to the right upper quadrant to continue suturing the upper border. The needle is directed out of the abdominal wall approximately one to two centimeters above the mesh border to maintain tautness.

The surgeon starts a new suture line in the right lower quadrant to complete the fixation of the mesh's right-sided border. An additional diagonal suture is placed down the midline of the mesh, although a midline suture is typically more common.
